mnemonics.title: ARGC = Argument Count
mnemonics.content: Remember: argc counts ALL arguments including the program name itself. Always at least 1.

mnemonics.title: ARGV = Argument Vector
mnemonics.content: argv is an array (vector) of character pointers pointing to argument strings. argv[0] is always the program name.

mnemonics.title: ENVP = Environment Pointer
mnemonics.content: envp points to environment variables in 'NAME=value' format, terminated by NULL.

mnemonics.title: Return to Parent
mnemonics.content: Think of main()'s return value as sending a status report back to the parent process/shell that launched you.

acronyms.usage: Number of command-line arguments passed to the program
Array of pointers to argument strings
Array of pointers to environment variable strings

visualTips.visualization: Imagine argv as a staircase: argv[0] is the first step (program name), argv[1] is the next, and argv[argc] is the landing (NULL). The actual strings are stored in a separate memory block.
Think of argc as the count button on an elevator, and argv as the floor buttons. argc tells you how many stops, argv tells you what's at each stop.

analogies.analogy: main() is like the front door of a house. Everyone must enter through it to start their activities inside. When you leave (return), you tell the host how the visit went (exit status).
argc is like counting guests at a party - it includes the host (program name) plus all attendees. You always have at least one (the host).
argv is like a list of name tags at a conference: argv[0] is the host's tag, argv[1] is first guest's, and argv[argc] is a blank tag marking the end of the list.
